exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Ask the person who has had the suspected TLoC, and any witnesses, to describe what happened before, during and after the event. Try to contact by telephone witnesses who are not present. Record details about: circumstances of the event person's posture immediately before loss of consciousness prodromal symptoms (such as sweating or feeling warm/hot) appearance (for example, whether eyes were open

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
1.5.4 Safety advice for people who have had TLoC
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: For people with an uncomplicated faint (uncomplicated vasovagal syncope) or situational syncope: explain the mechanisms causing their syncope advise on possible trigger events, and strategies for avoiding them; if the trigger events are unclear, advise people to keep a record of their symptoms, when they occur and what they were doing at the time, in order to understand what causes them to faint r

user_facing_items.final_wording: Assess and document: Use the person's history to distinguish people whose exercise-induced syncope occurred during exercise (when a cardiac arrhythmic cause is probable) from those whose syncope occurred shortly…
user_facing_items.population: Adults and young people aged 16 years and older with suspected transient loss of consciousness.
user_facing_items.setting: Initial assessment, emergency transfer, referral and specialist evaluation of transient loss of consciousness.

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
1.3.2 Diagnostic tests for different types of syncope
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Use the person's history to distinguish people whose exercise-induced syncope occurred during exercise (when a cardiac arrhythmic cause is probable) from those whose syncope occurred shortly after stopping exercise (when a vasovagal cause is more likely).

exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Clinical guideline Reference number: CG109 Published: 25 August 2010 Last updated: 21 November 2023 Download guidance (PDF)

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
1.1.3 Recording the event information and transfer of records
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Record carefully the information obtained from all accounts of the TLoC. Include paramedic records with this information. Give copies of the ECG record and the patient report form to the receiving clinician when care is transferred, and to the person who had the TLoC.

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: If blood pressure measurements do not confirm postural hypotension despite suggestive symptoms refer the person for further specialist cardiovascular assessment. [2023]

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Refer people who present with 1 or more of the following features (that is, features that are strongly suggestive of epileptic seizures) for an assessment by a specialist in epilepsy; the person should be seen by the specialist within 2 weeks (see NICE's guideline on epilepsies in children, young people and adults ): a bitten tongue head-turning to 1 side during TLoC no memory of abnormal behaviou

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Refer all people with TLoC (apart from the exceptions below) for a specialist cardiovascular assessment by the most appropriate local service. Exceptions are: people with a firm diagnosis, after the initial assessment, of: uncomplicated faint situational syncope postural hypotension people whose presentation is strongly suggestive of epileptic seizures.

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
1.4 If the cause of TLoC remains uncertain
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: If a person has persistent TLoC, consider psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES) or psychogenic pseudosyncope if, for example: the nature of the events changes over time there are multiple unexplained physical symptoms there are unusually prolonged events. The distinction between epilepsy and non-epileptic seizures is complex; therefore, refer for neurological assessment if either PNES or psych

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Give advice about eligibility to drive when a person first presents with TLoC. Please refer to the Driver and Vehicle Licensing Agency (DVLA) guidance on neurological disorders: assessing fitness to drive .

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Advise all people who have experienced TLoC that they must not drive while waiting for a specialist assessment. Following specialist assessment, the healthcare professional should advise the person of their obligations regarding reporting the TLoC event to the DVLA. Please refer to the DVLA guidance on neurological disorders: assessing fitness to drive .

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Advise people who have experienced TLoC of the implications of their episode for health and safety at work and any action they must take to ensure the safety of themselves and that of other people. Please refer to Health and Safety Executive's Health and Safety at Work etc Act 1974 .

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
1.5.4 Safety advice for people who have had TLoC
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Advise people waiting for a specialist cardiovascular assessment: what they should do if they have another event if appropriate, how they should modify their activity (for example, by avoiding physical exertion if relevant) and not to drive. Please refer to the DVLA guidance on neurological disorders: assessing fitness to drive .

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
1.1.2 Obtaining patient history, physical examination and tests
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: If during the initial assessment, there is suspicion of an underlying problem causing TLoC, or additional to TLoC, carry out relevant examinations and investigations (for example, check blood glucose levels if diabetic hypoglycaemia is suspected, or haemoglobin levels if anaemia or bleeding is suspected; see also recommendation 1.2.2.1 for information about the use of electroencephalogram [EEG] ).

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Suspect postural hypotension on the basis of the initial assessment when: there are no features suggesting an alternative diagnosis and the history is typical. See recommendations 1.1.5 to 1.1.7 of NICE's guideline on hypertension for details of how to measure and manage postural hypotension. [2010, amended 2023]

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
1.3.1 Assessment and assignment to type of syncope
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Carry out a specialist cardiovascular assessment as follows: Reassess the person's: detailed history of TLoC including any previous events medical history and any family history of cardiac disease or an inherited cardiac condition drug therapy at the time of TLoC and any subsequent changes. Conduct a clinical examination, including full cardiovascular examination and, if clinically appropriate, me

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
1.3.1 Assessment and assignment to type of syncope
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: For people with suspected structural heart disease, investigate appropriately (for example, cardiac imaging). Because other mechanisms for syncope are possible in this group, also consider investigating for a cardiac arrhythmic cause (as described in recommendation 1.3.2.4 ), and for postural hypotension (often caused/exacerbated by drug therapy – see recommendation 1.2.1.1 ) or for neurally media

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
1.3.2 Diagnostic tests for different types of syncope
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: If the mechanism for exercise-induced syncope is identified by exercise testing, carry out further investigation or treatment as appropriate in each individual clinical context. Otherwise, carry out further investigations assuming a suspected cardiac arrhythmic cause.

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
1.3.2 Diagnostic tests for different types of syncope
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: For people with a suspected cardiac arrhythmic cause of syncope, offer an ambulatory ECG and do not offer a tilt test as a first-line investigation. The type of ambulatory ECG offered should be chosen on the basis of the person's history (and, in particular, frequency) of TLoC. For people who have: TLoC at least several times a week, offer Holter monitoring (up to 48 hours if necessary). If no fur

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
1.3.2 Diagnostic tests for different types of syncope
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Do not offer a tilt test to people who have a diagnosis of vasovagal syncope on initial assessment.

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
1.3.2 Diagnostic tests for different types of syncope
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: For people with suspected vasovagal syncope with recurrent episodes of TLoC adversely affecting their quality of life, or representing a high risk of injury, consider a tilt test only to assess whether the syncope is accompanied by a severe cardioinhibitory response (usually asystole ).

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
1.3.2 Diagnostic tests for different types of syncope
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: For people with suspected carotid sinus syncope and for people with unexplained syncope who are aged 60 years or older, offer carotid sinus massage as a first-line investigation. This should be conducted in a controlled environment, with ECG recording, and with resuscitation equipment available.

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
1.3.2 Diagnostic tests for different types of syncope
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Diagnose carotid sinus syncope if carotid sinus massage reproduces syncope due to marked bradycardia/asystole and/or marked hypotension. Do not diagnose carotid sinus syncope if carotid sinus massage causes asymptomatic transient bradycardia or hypotension (see recommendation 1.3.2.9).

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
1.3.2 Diagnostic tests for different types of syncope
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: For all people with unexplained syncope (including after negative carotid sinus massage test in those for whom this is appropriate), offer ambulatory ECG (see recommendation 1.3.2.4 ). Do not offer a tilt test before the ambulatory ECG.

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
1.4 If the cause of TLoC remains uncertain
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Advise people who have experienced TLoC to try to record any future events (for example, a video recording or a detailed witness account of the event), particularly if the diagnosis is unclear or taking a history is difficult.

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: When communicating with the person who had TLoC, discuss the: possible causes of their TLoC benefits and risks of any tests they are offered results of tests they have had reasons for any further investigations they are offered nature and extent of uncertainty in the diagnosis.

user_facing_items.final_wording: Document relevant investigations: Diagnose situational syncope on the basis of the initial assessment when: there are no features from the initial assessment that suggest an alternative diagnosis and syncope is…

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
1.1.4 Making a judgement based on initial assessment
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Diagnose situational syncope on the basis of the initial assessment when: there are no features from the initial assessment that suggest an alternative diagnosis and syncope is clearly and consistently provoked by straining during micturition (usually while standing) or by coughing or swallowing.

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
1.1.4 Making a judgement based on initial assessment
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: If a diagnosis of uncomplicated faint or situational syncope is made, and there is nothing in the initial assessment to raise clinical or social concern, no further immediate management is required. If the presentation is not to the GP, the healthcare professional should: advise the person to take a copy of the patient report form and the ECG record to their GP inform the GP about the diagnosis, d

exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: If the person with suspected transient loss of consciousness (TLoC) has sustained an injury or they have not made a full recovery of consciousness, use clinical judgement to determine appropriate management and the urgency of treatment.

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
1.1.4 Making a judgement based on initial assessment
Red flags: people requiring urgent assessment and treatment
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: If TLoC is secondary to a condition that requires immediate action, use clinical judgement to determine appropriate management and the urgency of treatment.

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
1.3.2 Diagnostic tests for different types of syncope
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: When offering a person an implantable event recorder, provide one that has both patient-activated and automatic detection modes. Instruct the person and their family and/or carer how to operate the device. Advise the person that they should have prompt follow-up (data interrogation of the device) after they have any further TLoC. The timing of the follow-up is dependent on the storage on the devic

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
1.4 If the cause of TLoC remains uncertain
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: If after further assessment the cause of TLoC remains uncertain or the person has not responded to treatment, consider other causes including the possibility that more than 1 mechanism may coexist (for example, ictal arrhythmias ).

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
1.5.4 Safety advice for people who have had TLoC
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: For people with postural hypotension: explain the mechanisms causing their syncope discuss and review possible causes, especially drug therapy discuss the prognostic implications and treatment options available advise people what to do if they experience another TLoC.

user_facing_items.final_wording: Document management considerations: This is particularly important when communicating with a child or young person, or person with special communication needs.

exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: When recording a description of the suspected TLoC from the patient or a witness, take care to ensure that their communication and other needs are taken into account. This is particularly important when communicating with a child or young person, or person with special communication needs.

Determining whether the person had TLoC
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Use information gathered from all accounts of the suspected TLoC (see recommendation 1.1.1.2) to confirm whether or not TLoC has occurred. If this is uncertain it should be assumed that they had TLoC until proven otherwise. But, if the person did not have TLoC, instigate suitable management (for example, if the person is determined to have had a fall, rather than TLoC, see NICE's guideline on fall

user_facing_items.final_wording: Discuss with the patient: Offer advice to people waiting for specialist neurological assessment for their TLoC as recommended in NICE's guideline on epilepsies in children, young people and adults .

exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Offer advice to people waiting for specialist neurological assessment for their TLoC as recommended in NICE's guideline on epilepsies in children, young people and adults .

user_facing_items.final_wording: Check and document red flags: Treat as a red flag (see recommendation 1.1.4.2) if any of the following abnormalities are reported on the ECG printout: conduction abnormality (for example, complete right or left…
user_facing_items.population: Adults and young people aged 16 years and older with suspected transient loss of consciousness.
user_facing_items.setting: Initial assessment, emergency transfer, referral and specialist evaluation of transient loss of consciousness.

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
1.1.2 Obtaining patient history, physical examination and tests
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Record a 12-lead electrocardiogram (ECG) using automated interpretation. Treat as a red flag (see recommendation 1.1.4.2) if any of the following abnormalities are reported on the ECG printout: conduction abnormality (for example, complete right or left bundle branch block or any degree of heart block) evidence of a long or short QT interval or any ST segment or T wave abnormalities.

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
1.3.2 Diagnostic tests for different types of syncope
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: For people who have experienced syncope during exercise, offer urgent (within 7 days) exercise testing, unless there is a possible contraindication (such as suspected aortic stenosis or hypertrophic cardiomyopathy requiring initial assessment by imaging). Advise the person to refrain from exercise until informed otherwise following further assessment.

user_facing_items.final_wording: Consider referring within 24 hours for cardiovascular assessment, as above, anyone aged older than 65 years who has experienced TLoC without prodromal symptoms.
user_facing_items.population: Adults and young people aged 16 years and older with suspected transient loss of consciousness.
user_facing_items.setting: Initial assessment, emergency transfer, referral and specialist evaluation of transient loss of consciousness.

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
1.1.4 Making a judgement based on initial assessment
Red flags: people requiring urgent assessment and treatment
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Refer urgently for cardiovascular assessment, with the referral reviewed and prioritised by an appropriate specialist within 24 hours, anyone with TLoC who also has any of the following: an ECG abnormality (see recommendations 1.1.2.2 and 1.1.2.3) heart failure (history or physical signs) TLoC during exertion family history of sudden cardiac death in people aged younger than 40 years and/or an inh

user_facing_items.final_wording: Check and document red flags: If the person presents to the ambulance service, take them to the emergency department unless a diagnosis of an uncomplicated faint or situational syncope is clear.

evidence_records.final_wording: People have the right to be involved in discussions and make informed decisions about their care, as described in NICE's information on making decisions about your care . Making decisions using NICE guidelines explains how we use words to show the strength (or certainty) of our recommendations, and has information about prescribing medicines (including off-label use), professional guidelines, standards and laws (including on consent and mental capacity), and safeguarding. This guideline refers to different types of syncope. See the section on terms used in this guideline for definitions .

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
1.1.1 Gathering information about the event and initial decision making

evidence_records.population: Adults and young people aged 16 years and older with suspected transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.setting: Initial assessment, emergency transfer, referral and specialist evaluation of transient loss of consciousness.

evidence_records.final_wording: Ask the person who has had the suspected TLoC, and any witnesses, to describe what happened before, during and after the event. Try to contact by telephone witnesses who are not present. Record details about: circumstances of the event person's posture immediately before loss of consciousness prodromal symptoms (such as sweating or feeling warm/hot) appearance (for example, whether eyes were open or shut) and colour of the person during the event presence or absence of movement during the event (for example, limb-jerking and its duration) any tongue-biting (record whether the side or the tip of the tongue was bitten) injury occurring during the event (record site and severity) duration of the event (onset to regaining consciousness) presence or absence of confusion during the recovery period weakness down 1 side during the recovery period.

evidence_records.final_wording: Use information gathered from all accounts of the suspected TLoC (see recommendation 1.1.1.2) to confirm whether or not TLoC has occurred. If this is uncertain it should be assumed that they had TLoC until proven otherwise. But, if the person did not have TLoC, instigate suitable management (for example, if the person is determined to have had a fall, rather than TLoC, see NICE's guideline on falls in older people: assessing risk and prevention ).

exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Use information gathered from all accounts of the suspected TLoC (see recommendation 1.1.1.2) to confirm whether or not TLoC has occurred. If this is uncertain it should be assumed that they had TLoC until proven otherwise. But, if the person did not have TLoC, instigate suitable management (for example, if the person is determined to have had a fall, rather than TLoC, see NICE's guideline on fall

evidence_records.population: Adults and young people aged 16 years and older with suspected transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.setting: Initial assessment, emergency transfer, referral and specialist evaluation of transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.rationale: Directly reproduced from an exact evidence statement in a complete archetype-compatible pack.

evidence_records.final_wording: Assess and record: details of any previous TLoC, including number and frequency the person's medical history and any family history of cardiac disease (for example, personal history of heart disease and family history of sudden cardiac death) current medication that may have contributed to TLoC (for example, diuretics) vital signs (for example, pulse rate, respiratory rate and temperature) – repeat if clinically indicated lying and standing blood pressure if clinically appropriate other cardiovascular and neurological signs.

evidence_records.population: Adults and young people aged 16 years and older with suspected transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.setting: Initial assessment, emergency transfer, referral and specialist evaluation of transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.rationale: Directly reproduced from an exact evidence statement in a complete archetype-compatible pack.

evidence_records.final_wording: Record a 12-lead electrocardiogram (ECG) using automated interpretation. Treat as a red flag (see recommendation 1.1.4.2) if any of the following abnormalities are reported on the ECG printout: conduction abnormality (for example, complete right or left bundle branch block or any degree of heart block) evidence of a long or short QT interval or any ST segment or T wave abnormalities.

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s

Treat as a red flag (see recommendation 1.1.4.2) if any of the following abnormalities are reported on the ECG printout: conduction abnormality (for example, complete right or left bundle branch block or any degree of heart block) evidence of a long or short QT interval or any ST segment or T wave abnormalities.

evidence_records.population: Adults and young people aged 16 years and older with suspected transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.setting: Initial assessment, emergency transfer, referral and specialist evaluation of transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.rationale: Directly reproduced from an exact evidence statement in a complete archetype-compatible pack.

evidence_records.final_wording: If a 12-lead ECG with automated interpretation is not available, take a manual 12-lead ECG reading and have this reviewed by a healthcare professional trained and competent in identifying the following abnormalities: inappropriate persistent bradycardia any ventricular arrhythmia (including ventricular ectopic beats) long QT (corrected QT more than 450 ms) and short QT (corrected QT less than 350 ms) intervals Brugada syndrome ventricular pre-excitation (part of Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome) left or right ventricular hypertrophy abnormal T wave inversion pathological Q waves atrial arrhythmia (sustained) paced rhythm.

evidence_records.population: Adults and young people aged 16 years and older with suspected transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.setting: Initial assessment, emergency transfer, referral and specialist evaluation of transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.rationale: Directly reproduced from an exact evidence statement in a complete archetype-compatible pack.

evidence_records.final_wording: If during the initial assessment, there is suspicion of an underlying problem causing TLoC, or additional to TLoC, carry out relevant examinations and investigations (for example, check blood glucose levels if diabetic hypoglycaemia is suspected, or haemoglobin levels if anaemia or bleeding is suspected; see also recommendation 1.2.2.1 for information about the use of electroencephalogram [EEG] ).

evidence_records.setting: Initial assessment, emergency transfer, referral and specialist evaluation of transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.rationale: Directly reproduced from an exact evidence statement in a complete archetype-compatible pack.

evidence_records.final_wording: Record carefully the information obtained from all accounts of the TLoC. Include paramedic records with this information. evidence_records.final_wording: If TLoC is secondary to a condition that requires immediate action, use clinical judgement to determine appropriate management and the urgency of treatment.

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
1.1.4 Making a judgement based on initial assessment
Red flags: people requiring urgent assessment and treatment
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: If TLoC is secondary to a condition that requires immediate action, use clinical judgement to determine appropriate management and the urgency of treatment.

evidence_records.population: Adults and young people aged 16 years and older with suspected transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.setting: Initial assessment, emergency transfer, referral and specialist evaluation of transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.rationale: Directly reproduced from an exact evidence statement in a complete archetype-compatible pack.

evidence_records.final_wording: Refer urgently for cardiovascular assessment, with the referral reviewed and prioritised by an appropriate specialist within 24 hours, anyone with TLoC who also has any of the following: an ECG abnormality (see recommendations 1.1.2.2 and 1.1.2.3) heart failure (history or physical signs) TLoC during exertion family history of sudden cardiac death in people aged younger than 40 years and/or an inherited cardiac condition new or unexplained breathlessness a heart murmur. Consider referring within 24 hours for cardiovascular assessment, as above, anyone aged older than 65 years who has experienced TLoC without prodromal symptoms.

evidence_records.population: Adults and young people aged 16 years and older with suspected transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.setting: Initial assessment, emergency transfer, referral and specialist evaluation of transient loss of consciousness.

evidence_records.final_wording: Diagnose uncomplicated faint (uncomplicated vasovagal syncope ) on the basis of the initial assessment when: there are no features that suggest an alternative diagnosis (note that brief seizure activity can occur during uncomplicated faints and is not necessarily diagnostic of epilepsy) and there are features suggestive of uncomplicated faint (the 3 'P's) such as: P osture – prolonged standing, or similar episodes that have been prevented by lying down P rovoking factors (such as pain or a medical procedure) P rodromal symptoms (such as sweating or feeling warm/hot before TLoC).

evidence_records.population: Adults and young people aged 16 years and older with suspected transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.setting: Initial assessment, emergency transfer, referral and specialist evaluation of transient loss of consciousness.

evidence_records.final_wording: Diagnose situational syncope on the basis of the initial assessment when: there are no features from the initial assessment that suggest an alternative diagnosis and syncope is clearly and consistently provoked by straining during micturition (usually while standing) or by coughing or swallowing.

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s

evidence_records.population: Adults and young people aged 16 years and older with suspected transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.setting: Initial assessment, emergency transfer, referral and specialist evaluation of transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.rationale: Directly reproduced from an exact evidence statement in a complete archetype-compatible pack.

evidence_records.final_wording: If a diagnosis of uncomplicated faint or situational syncope is made, and there is nothing in the initial assessment to raise clinical or social concern, no further immediate management is required. If the presentation is not to the GP, the healthcare professional should: advise the person to take a copy of the patient report form and the ECG record to their GP inform the GP about the diagnosis, directly if possible; if an ECG has not been recorded, the GP should arrange an ECG (and its interpretation as described in recommendation 1.1.2.3 ) within 3 days.

evidence_records.final_wording: Refer people who present with 1 or more of the following features (that is, features that are strongly suggestive of epileptic seizures) for an assessment by a specialist in epilepsy; the person should be seen by the specialist within 2 weeks (see NICE's guideline on epilepsies in children, young people and adults ): a bitten tongue head-turning to 1 side during TLoC no memory of abnormal behaviour that was witnessed before, during or after TLoC by someone else unusual posturing prolonged limb-jerking (note that brief seizure-like activity can often occur during uncomplicated faints) confusion following the event prodromal déjà vu , or jamais vu . evidence_records.population: Adults and young people aged 16 years and older with suspected transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.setting: Initial assessment, emergency transfer, referral and specialist evaluation of transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.rationale: Directly reproduced from an exact evidence statement in a complete archetype-compatible pack.

evidence_records.final_wording: Refer all people with TLoC (apart from the exceptions below) for a specialist cardiovascular assessment by the most appropriate local service. Exceptions are: people with a firm diagnosis, after the initial assessment, of: uncomplicated faint situational syncope postural hypotension people whose presentation is strongly suggestive of epileptic seizures.

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s

evidence_records.population: Adults and young people aged 16 years and older with suspected transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.setting: Initial assessment, emergency transfer, referral and specialist evaluation of transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.rationale: Directly reproduced from an exact evidence statement in a complete archetype-compatible pack.

evidence_records.final_wording: Carry out a specialist cardiovascular assessment as follows: Reassess the person's: detailed history of TLoC including any previous events medical history and any family history of cardiac disease or an inherited cardiac condition drug therapy at the time of TLoC and any subsequent changes. Conduct a clinical examination, including full cardiovascular examination and, if clinically appropriate, measurement of lying and standing blood pressure. Repeat 12-lead ECG and obtain and examine previous ECG recordings. On the basis of this assessment, assign the person to 1 of the following suspected causes of syncope. suspected structural heart disease suspected cardiac arrhythmic suspected neurally mediated unexplained. Offer further testing as directed by recommendations 1.3.2.1 to 1.3.2.10 or other tests as clinically appropriate.

evidence_records.population: Adults and young people aged 16 years and older with suspected transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.setting: Initial assessment, emergency transfer, referral and specialist evaluation of transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.rationale: Directly reproduced from an exact evidence statement in a complete archetype-compatible pack.

evidence_records.final_wording: For people with suspected structural heart disease, investigate appropriately (for example, cardiac imaging). Because other mechanisms for syncope are possible in this group, also consider investigating for a cardiac arrhythmic cause (as described in recommendation 1.3.2.4 ), and for postural hypotension (often caused/exacerbated by drug therapy – see recommendation 1.2.1.1 ) or for neurally mediated syncope (see recommendations 1.3.2.5 and 1.3.2.6 ).

evidence_records.population: Adults and young people aged 16 years and older with suspected transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.setting: Initial assessment, emergency transfer, referral and specialist evaluation of transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.rationale: Directly reproduced from an exact evidence statement in a complete archetype-compatible pack.

evidence_records.final_wording: Use the person's history to distinguish people whose exercise-induced syncope occurred during exercise (when a cardiac arrhythmic cause is probable) from those whose syncope occurred shortly after stopping exercise (when a vasovagal cause is more likely).

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
1.3.2 Diagnostic tests for different types of syncope
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Use the person's history to distinguish people whose exercise-induced syncope occurred during exercise (when a cardiac arrhythmic cause is probable) from those whose syncope occurred shortly after stopping exercise (when a vasovagal cause is more likely).

evidence_records.population: Adults and young people aged 16 years and older with suspected transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.setting: Initial assessment, emergency transfer, referral and specialist evaluation of transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.rationale: Directly reproduced from an exact evidence statement in a complete archetype-compatible pack.

evidence_records.final_wording: For people who have experienced syncope during exercise, offer urgent (within 7 days) exercise testing, unless there is a possible contraindication (such as suspected aortic stenosis or hypertrophic cardiomyopathy requiring initial assessment by imaging). Advise the person to refrain from exercise until informed otherwise following further assessment.

evidence_records.final_wording: If the mechanism for exercise-induced syncope is identified by exercise testing, carry out further investigation or treatment as appropriate in each individual clinical context. Otherwise, carry out further investigations assuming a suspected cardiac arrhythmic cause.

evidence_records.population: Adults and young people aged 16 years and older with suspected transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.setting: Initial assessment, emergency transfer, referral and specialist evaluation of transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.rationale: Directly reproduced from an exact evidence statement in a complete archetype-compatible pack.

evidence_records.final_wording: For people with a suspected cardiac arrhythmic cause of syncope, offer an ambulatory ECG and do not offer a tilt test as a first-line investigation. The type of ambulatory ECG offered should be chosen on the basis of the person's history (and, in particular, frequency) of TLoC. For people who have: TLoC at least several times a week, offer Holter monitoring (up to 48 hours if necessary). If no further TLoC occurs during the monitoring period, offer an external event recorder that provides continuous recording with the facility for the patient to indicate when a symptomatic event has occurred. TLoC every 1 to 2 weeks, offer an external event recorder. If the person experiences further TLoC outside the period of external event recording, offer an implantable event recorder . TLoC infrequently (less than once every 2 weeks), offer an implantable event recorder. A Holter monitor should not u

evidence_records.final_wording: Do not offer a tilt test to people who have a diagnosis of vasovagal syncope on initial assessment.

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s

evidence_records.population: Adults and young people aged 16 years and older with suspected transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.setting: Initial assessment, emergency transfer, referral and specialist evaluation of transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.rationale: Directly reproduced from an exact evidence statement in a complete archetype-compatible pack.

evidence_records.final_wording: For people with suspected vasovagal syncope with recurrent episodes of TLoC adversely affecting their quality of life, or representing a high risk of injury, consider a tilt test only to assess whether the syncope is accompanied by a severe cardioinhibitory response (usually asystole ).

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s

evidence_records.population: Adults and young people aged 16 years and older with suspected transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.setting: Initial assessment, emergency transfer, referral and specialist evaluation of transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.rationale: Directly reproduced from an exact evidence statement in a complete archetype-compatible pack.

evidence_records.final_wording: For people with suspected carotid sinus syncope and for people with unexplained syncope who are aged 60 years or older, offer carotid sinus massage as a first-line investigation. This should be conducted in a controlled environment, with ECG recording, and with resuscitation equipment available.

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s

evidence_records.population: Adults and young people aged 16 years and older with suspected transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.setting: Initial assessment, emergency transfer, referral and specialist evaluation of transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.rationale: Directly reproduced from an exact evidence statement in a complete archetype-compatible pack.

evidence_records.final_wording: Diagnose carotid sinus syncope if carotid sinus massage reproduces syncope due to marked bradycardia/asystole and/or marked hypotension. Do not diagnose carotid sinus syncope if carotid sinus massage causes asymptomatic transient bradycardia or hypotension (see recommendation 1.3.2.9).

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s

evidence_records.population: Adults and young people aged 16 years and older with suspected transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.setting: Initial assessment, emergency transfer, referral and specialist evaluation of transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.rationale: Directly reproduced from an exact evidence statement in a complete archetype-compatible pack.

evidence_records.final_wording: For all people with unexplained syncope (including after negative carotid sinus massage test in those for whom this is appropriate), offer ambulatory ECG (see recommendation 1.3.2.4 ). Do not offer a tilt test before the ambulatory ECG.

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
1.3.2 Diagnostic tests for different types of syncope
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: For all people with unexplained syncope (including after negative carotid sinus massage test in those for whom this is appropriate), offer ambulatory ECG (see recommendation 1.3.2.4 ). Do not offer a tilt test before the ambulatory ECG.

evidence_records.population: Adults and young people aged 16 years and older with suspected transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.setting: Initial assessment, emergency transfer, referral and specialist evaluation of transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.rationale: Directly reproduced from an exact evidence statement in a complete archetype-compatible pack.

evidence_records.final_wording: When offering a person an implantable event recorder, provide one that has both patient-activated and automatic detection modes. Instruct the person and their family and/or carer how to operate the device. Advise the person that they should have prompt follow-up (data interrogation of the device) after they have any further TLoC. The timing of the follow-up is dependent on the storage on the device and the condition of the person.

evidence_records.population: Adults and young people aged 16 years and older with suspected transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.setting: Initial assessment, emergency transfer, referral and specialist evaluation of transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.rationale: Directly reproduced from an exact evidence statement in a complete archetype-compatible pack.

evidence_records.final_wording: If a person has persistent TLoC, consider psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES) or psychogenic pseudosyncope if, for example: the nature of the events changes over time there are multiple unexplained physical symptoms there are unusually prolonged events. The distinction between epilepsy and non-epileptic seizures is complex; therefore, refer for neurological assessment if either PNES or psychogenic pseudosyncope is suspected.

evidence_records.final_wording: Advise people who have experienced TLoC to try to record any future events (for example, a video recording or a detailed witness account of the event), particularly if the diagnosis is unclear or taking a history is difficult.

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
1.4 If the cause of TLoC remains uncertain

evidence_records.population: Adults and young people aged 16 years and older with suspected transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.setting: Initial assessment, emergency transfer, referral and specialist evaluation of transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.rationale: Directly reproduced from an exact evidence statement in a complete archetype-compatible pack.

evidence_records.final_wording: If after further assessment the cause of TLoC remains uncertain or the person has not responded to treatment, consider other causes including the possibility that more than 1 mechanism may coexist (for example, ictal arrhythmias ).

evidence_records.rationale: Directly reproduced from an exact evidence statement in a complete archetype-compatible pack.

evidence_records.final_wording: When communicating with the person who had TLoC, discuss the: possible causes of their TLoC benefits and risks of any tests they are offered results of tests they have had reasons for any further investigations they are offered nature and extent of uncertainty in the diagnosis.

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s

evidence_records.population: Adults and young people aged 16 years and older with suspected transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.setting: Initial assessment, emergency transfer, referral and specialist evaluation of transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.rationale: Directly reproduced from an exact evidence statement in a complete archetype-compatible pack.

evidence_records.final_wording: Give advice about eligibility to drive when a person first presents with TLoC. Please refer to the Driver and Vehicle Licensing Agency (DVLA) guidance on neurological disorders: assessing fitness to drive .

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Give advice about eligibility to drive when a person first presents with TLoC. Please refer to the Driver and Vehicle Licensing Agency (DVLA) guidance on neurological disorders: assessing fitness to drive .

evidence_records.population: Adults and young people aged 16 years and older with suspected transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.setting: Initial assessment, emergency transfer, referral and specialist evaluation of transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.rationale: Directly reproduced from an exact evidence statement in a complete archetype-compatible pack.

evidence_records.final_wording: Advise all people who have experienced TLoC that they must not drive while waiting for a specialist assessment. Following specialist assessment, the healthcare professional should advise the person of their obligations regarding reporting the TLoC event to the DVLA. Please refer to the DVLA guidance on neurological disorders: assessing fitness to drive .

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s

evidence_records.population: Adults and young people aged 16 years and older with suspected transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.setting: Initial assessment, emergency transfer, referral and specialist evaluation of transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.rationale: Directly reproduced from an exact evidence statement in a complete archetype-compatible pack.

evidence_records.final_wording: Advise people who have experienced TLoC of the implications of their episode for health and safety at work and any action they must take to ensure the safety of themselves and that of other people. Please refer to Health and Safety Executive's Health and Safety at Work etc Act 1974 .

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Advise people who have experienced TLoC of the implications of their episode for health and safety at work and any action they must take to ensure the safety of themselves and that of other people. Please refer to Health and Safety Executive's Health and Safety at Work etc Act 1974 .

evidence_records.population: Adults and young people aged 16 years and older with suspected transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.setting: Initial assessment, emergency transfer, referral and specialist evaluation of transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.rationale: Directly reproduced from an exact evidence statement in a complete archetype-compatible pack.

evidence_records.final_wording: For people with an uncomplicated faint (uncomplicated vasovagal syncope) or situational syncope: explain the mechanisms causing their syncope advise on possible trigger events, and strategies for avoiding them; if the trigger events are unclear, advise people to keep a record of their symptoms, when they occur and what they were doing at the time, in order to understand what causes them to faint reassure them that their prognosis is good advise them to consult their GP if they experience further TLoC, particularly if this differs from their recent episode.

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
1.5.4 Safety advice for people who have had TLoC
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: For people with an uncomplicated faint (uncomplicated vasovagal syncope) or situational syncope: explain the mechanisms causing their syncope advise on possible trigger events, and strategies for avoiding them; if the trigger events are unclear, advise people to keep a record of their symptoms, when they occur and what they were doing at the time, in order to understand what causes them to faint r

evidence_records.population: Adults and young people aged 16 years and older with suspected transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.setting: Initial assessment, emergency transfer, referral and specialist evaluation of transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.rationale: Directly reproduced from an exact evidence statement in a complete archetype-compatible pack.

evidence_records.final_wording: For people with postural hypotension: explain the mechanisms causing their syncope discuss and review possible causes, especially drug therapy discuss the prognostic implications and treatment options available advise people what to do if they experience another TLoC.

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
1.5.4 Safety advice for people who have had TLoC
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: For people with postural hypotension: explain the mechanisms causing their syncope discuss and review possible causes, especially drug therapy discuss the prognostic implications and treatment options available advise people what to do if they experience another TLoC.

evidence_records.population: Adults and young people aged 16 years and older with suspected transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.setting: Initial assessment, emergency transfer, referral and specialist evaluation of transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.rationale: Directly reproduced from an exact evidence statement in a complete archetype-compatible pack.

evidence_records.final_wording: Advise people waiting for a specialist cardiovascular assessment: what they should do if they have another event if appropriate, how they should modify their activity (for example, by avoiding physical exertion if relevant) and not to drive. Please refer to the DVLA guidance on neurological disorders: assessing fitness to drive .

heading_path: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
1.5.4 Safety advice for people who have had TLoC

evidence_records.setting: Initial assessment, emergency transfer, referral and specialist evaluation of transient loss of consciousness.
evidence_records.rationale: Directly reproduced from an exact evidence statement in a complete archetype-compatible pack.

evidence_records.final_wording: Offer advice to people waiting for specialist neurological assessment for their TLoC as recommended in NICE's guideline on epilepsies in children, young people and adults .
